title: The Context Window as a Budget
concept: Treating tokens as a scarce, costed resource that must be allocated deliberately across competing content.

exercises.prompt: In the last lesson, we learned a context window is made of distinct layers. Which of these is one of those layers?
exercises.explain: Retrieved documents are one of the key layers that can go into a context window, alongside system instructions, tool outputs, conversation history, and scratch memory. The model's weights and GPU speed are part of the underlying hardware/model, not content placed into the context window.

cards.title: You Can't Bring Everything
cards.body: Imagine trying to cram your entire house into one backpack for a hike. Something has to stay behind. Context windows force the same hard choices, every single time you send a prompt.

cards.title: Tokens as a Scarce Resource
cards.body: A context window has a fixed maximum number of tokens, and every token you use costs money and processing time. Because space is limited, you must deliberately decide which content deserves a spot instead of stuffing everything in.

cards.title: Packing a Suitcase for a Trip
cards.body: Your suitcase has limited space, so you can't bring your whole closet. You pick the most useful outfits and leave the rest at home. A context window works the same way: you pick the most useful tokens and leave the rest out.

cards.title: Splitting Up the Budget
cards.body: Say your window holds 8,000 tokens. You might allocate 300 for system instructions, 2,000 for retrieved documents, 1,000 for tool outputs, 3,000 for conversation history, and reserve 1,700 for the model's response. If history grows past its share, you trim old turns to stay on budget.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger Window = No More Planning Needed
cards.body: Even with huge context windows (like 100K+ tokens), stuffing in everything still costs more money, slows responses, and can bury the important details in noise. A bigger suitcase still needs packing decisions, just at a bigger scale.

cards.title: Unlimited Stuffing vs. Deliberate Budgeting
cards.body: One approach throws everything into the prompt and hopes for the best. The other assigns each type of content a token allowance and trims when it runs over.

left.text: Full history, every document, all tool logs crammed in — expensive, slow, and the model gets lost in the clutter.

right.text: Each section gets a token limit; old or less relevant content is trimmed or summarized to protect space for what matters most.

exercises.prompt: Why can't you just always include every retrieved document and the full conversation history in the context window?

options.text: Because the context window has a fixed token limit, and more tokens also cost more money and slow down responses
Because language models refuse to read more than one document at a time
Because conversation history is not allowed in context windows
Because system instructions must always take up 90% of the window

exercises.explain: Context windows have a maximum token capacity, and using more tokens costs more money and processing time. That's why engineers must be selective, keeping only what actually helps the model answer well.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Since tokens cost money and are limited, context engineers should decide in advance how much space each part of the prompt (instructions, documents, history) is allowed to use.
exercises.explain: True. Planning a token budget ahead of time prevents any one part (like a runaway conversation history) from crowding out other important content, and keeps costs and response times predictable.

exercises.sentence: Because every token costs money and space, engineers must set a token budget for each part of the context window.
exercises.explain: A 'budget' is the right word because it captures the idea of deliberately allocating a limited resource (tokens) across competing needs, just like money in a household budget.

exercises.prompt: Which of these three descriptions of 'budgeting' a context window sounds like it was written by an AI trying to sound impressive but missing real understanding?

options.text: We allocate approximately 15% of tokens to instructions, 40% to retrieved context, and reserve the remainder for generation, adjusting dynamically based on query complexity.
Context budgeting represents a paradigmatic synergy of holistic token utilization, wherein the model transcends traditional memory constraints through emergent optimization.
We keep track of how many tokens each part of the prompt uses, and trim conversation history first if we're running out of space.

exercises.explain: Option B is full of vague, flashy buzzwords ('paradigmatic synergy', 'transcends traditional memory constraints', 'emergent optimization') that don't actually explain any real mechanism. The other two options describe concrete, specific budgeting decisions, which is what real context engineering looks like.

exercises.prompt: Your app has a 4,000-token context window. The system instructions take 500 tokens, the conversation history is growing every turn, and you need room for retrieved documents and the model's answer. What's the BEST context engineering move?

options.text: Let the conversation history grow forever since more history always helps
Set a token budget for each part (instructions, history, documents, answer space) and trim or summarize the parts that go over
Remove the system instructions first since they are the least important part
Always use the maximum context window size available, regardless of cost or need

exercises.explain: Treating the context window as a budget means every section gets an intentional allocation, not an unlimited free-for-all. History has to be trimmed or summarized once it eats into space needed for fresh documents and the model's own answer. Cutting instructions is risky since they steer behavior, and just maxing out window size ignores that bigger windows cost more money and slow responses. Good context engineering means deciding in advance how many tokens each layer deserves, and enforcing that budget as the conversation grows.
